title: Formatter and linter

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Formatter and linter; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Engineering; August 2023; section applicable to Formatter and linter
sources.claim: A formatter applies mechanical layout rules, while a linter reports configured syntactic, semantic, safety, or maintainability patterns; both must run with pinned project configuration.

sources.locator: NIST Secure Software Development Framework; SP 800-218 v1.1; section applicable to Formatter and linter
sources.claim: A formatter applies mechanical layout rules, while a linter reports configured syntactic, semantic, safety, or maintainability patterns; both must run with pinned project configuration.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A formatter applies mechanical layout rules, while a linter reports configured syntactic, semantic, safety, or maintainability patterns; both must run with pinned project configuration.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Formatting changes are separated from a logic fix so reviewers can see behavior clearly while CI enforces the same formatter version. Your goal is to configure and apply formatter and linter checks to changed files. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Dependencies and reproducible builds; Commits and history; Build and task commands; Diffs and patch review. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A formatter applies mechanical layout rules, while a linter reports configured syntactic, semantic, safety, or maintainability patterns; both must run with pinned project configuration. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: A clean lint result is not proof of correctness, rules can conflict or produce false positives, and automatic fixes can change behavior in some tools. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Formatter and linter are synonyms, and code that passes both cannot contain bugs or security flaws.

includes: Automate style and static checks through one documented command with minimal unrelated churn.
Configure and apply formatter and linter checks to changed files.
excludes: A clean lint result is not proof of correctness, rules can conflict or produce false positives, and automatic fixes can change behavior in some tools.

outcomes: Configure and apply formatter and linter checks to changed files.

examples.title: Formatter and linter: direct case
examples.situation: Formatting changes are separated from a logic fix so reviewers can see behavior clearly while CI enforces the same formatter version.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: Formatting changes are separated from a logic fix so reviewers can see behavior clearly while CI enforces the same formatter version.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Use formatting for consistent presentation and linting for bounded static checks, review automatic changes, and retain tests and human reasoning for behavior.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Use formatting for consistent presentation and linting for bounded static checks, review automatic changes, and retain tests and human reasoning for behavior.

examples.title: Formatter and linter: boundary case
examples.situation: A linter warns about an unhandled promise; the learner understands the failure path before applying a fix instead of disabling the rule globally.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A linter warns about an unhandled promise; the learner understands the failure path before applying a fix instead of disabling the rule globally.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: A clean lint result is not proof of correctness, rules can conflict or produce false positives, and automatic fixes can change behavior in some tools.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Formatter and linter are synonyms, and code that passes both cannot contain bugs or security flaws.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Configure and apply formatter and linter checks to changed files.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Formatter and linter are synonyms, and code that passes both cannot contain bugs or security flaws.
misconceptions.correction: Use formatting for consistent presentation and linting for bounded static checks, review automatic changes, and retain tests and human reasoning for behavior.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Formatter and linter are synonyms, and code that passes both cannot contain bugs or security flaws.

analogy.example: A controlled investigation can model Formatter and linter: preserve evidence, state a hypothesis, inspect one clue, automate repeatable checks, and narrow the change point.
usefulFor: Linking debugging, static checks, history search, and automated local gates
limitations: Debuggers and hooks can alter timing or execute code, and tool findings remain bounded evidence rather than proof.

recap.summary: You should now be able to configure and apply formatter and linter checks to changed files. Keep this boundary in view: A clean lint result is not proof of correctness, rules can conflict or produce false positives, and automatic fixes can change behavior in some tools.

Why is this statement incorrect: Formatter and linter are synonyms, and code that passes both cannot contain bugs or security flaws.
Apply the model to this case from memory: A linter warns about an unhandled promise; the learner understands the failure path before applying a fix instead of disabling the rule globally.
